channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran’s Supreme Leader Remains Absent, a Void at the Top of the Regime
item.description: Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei did not appear at his father’s funeral this week, fueling speculation about his physical condition and leaving a power vacuum in a divided country.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A pro-government demonstration in Tehran in May, with a banner showing Iran’s supreme leader, Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei.

item.title: The U.S. and Iran Are Fighting Again. Is the Cease-Fire Over?
item.description: Eric Schmitt, a New York Times National Security correspondent, analyzes recent skirmishes between the U.S. and Iran and whether they threaten ongoing negotiations for a peace deal.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt, Gilad Thaler, Jon Miller, Rafaela Balster, Stephanie Swart, Jon Hazell and Whitney Shefte
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Bombing Iran Didn’t Work for Trump. Neither Did a Tentative Cease-Fire. Is There a Plan C?
item.description: The administration appears to be reverting to an all-stick, no-carrot approach. But it has yet to answer why it believes economic warfare and bombing will yield a different result this time.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump at the White House on Monday. The United States has bombed more than 170 Iranian military targets over two nights this week as a cease-fire unraveled.

item.title: Mediators Trying to Pull U.S. and Iran Back From Brink, Officials Say
item.description: Days of strikes by the two countries have given way to an uneasy pause as Qatari mediators in Iran seek to salvage the tattered truce.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon and Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, as seen from Musandam, Oman, on Thursday.

item.title: How Trump Is Trying to Tip the Scales for the Midterms, and Why Grocery Stores Are Scrambling
item.description: Plus, the Friday news quiz.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump is using the powers of his office to try to reshape the rules governing the midterms and future elections.

item.title: New Air Force One Lacks Defensive Countermeasures of Previous Model, Officials Say
item.description: Experts said the lack of such capabilities poses a potential risk when the president travels overseas. The White House defended the aircraft’s safety.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager, Eric Lipton, Adam Goldman, Eric Schmitt and Julian E. Barnes
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The new presidential jet before its maiden flight as Air Force One last week.

item.title: Trump Administration Fires Members of Independent Election Group
item.description: The firings and a resignation render the Election Assistance Commission useless. The moves come as President Trump seeks to impose control over how ballots will be counted in the midterms.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green
item.media:description: The White House confirmed that it had dismissed members of the Election Assistance Commission, a bipartisan group tasked with helping states carry out elections.

item.title: Witnesses of ICE Killing in Houston Dispute the Official Account
item.description: The agency said a Mexican resident driving a van tried to ram agents before they shot him dead. A lawyer for three passengers said that was untrue.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: The site where a federal immigration officer shot Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Houston.

item.title: What We Know About the ICE Shooting of Lorenzo Salgado Araujo
item.description: Mr. Araujo was a father, a husband and a business owner who had moved to the United States 35 years ago from Mexico.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: A makeshift memorial for Lorenzo Salgado Araujo near the site where he was killed in Houston.

item.title: Claims of Poor Conditions in ICE Facility Are Bolstered by Health Inspectors
item.description: Detainees at the Delaney Hall center in Newark have complained for months about dirty, unhealthy living conditions. Documents echo their claims.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ley, Claire Fahy, Mark Bonamo and Nicole Hong
item.media:description: Delaney Hall has emerged as a flashpoint in President Trump’s immigration crackdown.

item.title: Why Some Women in Maine Are Mourning the End of Graham Platner’s Campaign
item.description: They believe the accuser, but they also grieve the demise of a campaign that promised that politics could be different — and they blame those who failed to find a less flawed candidate.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Democrats at every level of the party assumed that women who had supported Mr. Platner would be thrilled that he was being pushed out of the race following an accusation of rape.

item.title: China Launches Reusable Rocket, Clearing a Key Hurdle in Race With SpaceX
item.description: The launch and recovery of the Long March 10B could represent a long-awaited breakthrough for Chinese satellite companies.
item.dc:creator: Selam Gebrekidan and Pei-Lin Wu
item.media:credit: Visual China Group, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A Long March 10B rocket blasting off from the Wenchang spaceport in Hainan, China, on Friday.

item.title: How Volkswagen’s Troubles Were Made in China
item.description: The German auto giant’s push into China powered it for decades, but now the company faces fierce competition from Chinese automakers in markets around the world.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:credit: Qilai Shen for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Volkswagen booth at the Shanghai auto show. For many years, China generated at least half of the German car company’s profits.

item.title: Nine Years Later, They’re Still Restoring a House Bought at Auction
item.description: First-time buyers from the Bay Area won an abandoned house at a Kingston, N.Y. tax auction. Eight years later, they’re still restoring it.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Marvar and Lauren Lancaster
item.media:description: Aimee Gardner and David Linnard on the front porch of their “Disaster Mansion,” which they bought for $53,500 and have been renovating for years.

item.title: Bayeux Tapestry Arrives in U.K. After Maximum-Security Operation
item.description: The 11th-century tapestry will go on display at the British Museum in September. Tickets for the exhibition have already been selling rapidly.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: The tapestry in Bayeux, France, in 2018. British museums and officials have repeatedly asked to borrow it.

item.title: How American Socialism Changed, and Stormed the Democratic Party
item.description: The movement was better at critiquing the system than reshaping it. But it has never had this much mainstream political power.
item.dc:creator: Jia Lynn Yang
item.media:description: Senator Bernie Sanders of Vermont, left, and Mayor Zohran Mamdani of New York at a rally last month in Brooklyn.

item.title: How Terrorist Groups Are Using A.I. to Gain an Edge in Battle
item.description: A.I. chatbots are not just a propaganda tool for violent extremists but are aiding in bomb construction and attack planning, new research finds.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz and Eric Schmitt
item.category: Center for Strategic and International Studies
Islamic State in Iraq and Syria (ISIS)
item.media:description: Nigerian soldiers training at a military base in Monguno, Nigeria, last year amid a surge in attacks by jihadists. Extremist groups like Boko Haram are turning to A.I. for tactical on-the-ground advantages, highlighting a broader challenge for the A.I. industry.

item.title: A.I. Has Rewarded Investors. It May Now Pose Their Greatest Risk.
item.description: Near the midpoint of the year, stocks and bonds both report good returns. But the global stock market has become highly concentrated, our columnist says.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer

item.title: At Least 12 People Die in Southern Spain Wildfire
item.description: More than 20 people were still missing after the blaze in Andalusia, one of the deadliest on record in the country. Many of the victims were foreign tourists, the Spanish authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Carlos Barragán
item.media:credit: Infoca, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A still from a video provided by the Andalusia Wildfire Service of the wildfire in Los Gallardos district, Spain, on Friday.

item.title: Fighting Fires With Figures, These Experts Are Trying to Stop Europe From Burning
item.description: A team of engineers, foresters and scientists is helping the continent prepare for wildfires from a giant science park in Italy. Their arsenal includes satellites, weather models and expert analysis.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek
item.media:description: Firefighters in Patras, Greece, in August. National authorities fight fires within their borders, but the European Union helps coordinate responses and sends additional resources to places in need.

item.title: How Olivia Wilde Builds Comic Tension in ‘The Invite’
item.description: The director and actress discusses a tense scene from her dinner-party comedy, in which she stars with Seth Rogen, Penélope Cruz and Edward Norton.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy

item.title: ‘The Invite’ Review: Who’s Afraid of a Last-Minute Dinner Party?
item.description: In this witty comedy, Seth Rogen and Olivia Wilde star as a millennial couple whose tense marriage gets a wild jolt from their neighbors.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: Jerrold Nadler: Penn Station Won’t Fund Itself
item.description: Penn Station needs massive fixes, but the Trump administration is going about it all wrong.
item.dc:creator: Jerrold L. Nadler

item.title: The Very Good and Very Bad News on Climate
item.description: Advancements in renewable energy are paving the way for a new climate politics. The environmentalist Bill McKibben articulates some of the possibilities in this new era of energy abundance.
item.dc:creator: Ezra Klein and Jack McCordick

item.title: The Housing Crisis
item.description: Happy Friday. I want to tell you about a rare instance of bipartisan cooperation in Congress.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:description: Speaker Mike Johnson in the Capitol late last month.

item.title: Ronny Chieng Tackles Trump's Two Air Force Ones
item.description: “Is that why there’s no gas in the world anymore? Because the president flies with an extra emotional support plane?” Chieng said on “The Daily Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
